file_name: Rojas et al. - 2021 - Hybrid model of convolutional neural network and support vector machine to classify basal cell carci.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper proposes and evaluates a hybrid model combining a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and a Support Vector Machine (SVM) to classify Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC), a type of skin cancer. The CNN extracts features using 4 convolution blocks with 32, 64, and 128 filters, which are then passed to an L1-SVM classifier. The study reports performance metrics (precision, accuracy, recall, F1-score) for this AI-based diagnostic system.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper proposes and evaluates a novel hybrid model combining a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and a Support Vector Machine (SVM) to classify Basal Cell Carcinoma. It includes original methodology, experimental evaluation with specific performance metrics (precision, accuracy, recall, F1-score), and a comparison against a classic model, which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: Hybrid Model of Convolutional Neural Network and Support Vector Machine to Classify Basal Cell Carcinoma
brief_summary: This study proposes a hybrid model combining a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and a Support Vector Machine (SVM) to classify Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) using dermatoscopic images. The CNN component consists of four convolution blocks with 32, 64, and 128 filters for feature extraction, which are then passed to an L1-SVM classifier. The model was evaluated on the HAM10000 dataset, which contained 19,267 dermatoscopic images. Performance was measured using precision, accuracy, recall, and F1-score metrics, achieving average results of 96.20% for all four indicators. These results demonstrate that the hybrid CNN+SVM model outperforms the classic Softmax-based CNN model, which achieved approximately 95.66% across the same metrics. The authors conclude that the hybrid approach offers better generalization and convergence for BCC classification.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target of the classification task. The study uses the HAM10000 dataset, specifically focusing on BCC images (labeled as BCC vs Non-BCC in the experimental setup).

The negative class in the binary classification task. The paper notes that the HAM10000 dataset contains images of BCC and other skin lesions (non-BCC). It also mentions that 99% of skin cancer cases are non-melanoma, with BCC being the majority of those.
Mentioned in the introduction to provide context on skin cancer prevalence (1% of cases) and to distinguish BCC from melanoma. Not a target class in the experiments.

Mentioned in the context of other studies (e.g., [8]) that used datasets with normal dermatoscopy images. The current study uses HAM10000 which is described as containing BCC and other lesion types, but 'normal' is not explicitly listed as a specific class label in the results tables, which use 'Non-BCC'.

all_task_types.details: Binary classification of dermatoscopic images to distinguish between Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) and Non-BCC lesions using a hybrid CNN+SVM model. The study reports precision, accuracy, recall, and F1-score metrics for this diagnostic task.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A dataset of dermatoscopic images of pigmented skin lesions from two different sites: the Department of Dermatology of the Austrian University of Medicine of Austria and Skin Cancer Practice of Cliff Rosendahlen in Queensland, Australia.

use.training: Used for training the CNN feature extractor and SVM classifier (10,837 images).
use.testing: Used for model evaluation with metrics of precision, accuracy, recall, and F1-score (4,817 images).
use.other: The original dataset contained 10,015 images with 7 classes (akiec, bcc, bkl, df, nv, mel, vasc), but was filtered and relabeled for this binary classification task (bcc vs nobcc) and augmented.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To extract True Positives (TP), True Negatives (TN), False Negatives (FN), and False Positives (FP) for calculating precision, accuracy, recall, and F1-score.
To evaluate and compare the performance of the classic CNN+Softmax model and the hybrid CNN+SVM model on the test set.
To monitor training convergence and evaluate overfitting by plotting losses against epochs for both models.
To qualitatively assess generalization capacity and overfitting by comparing the loss trends of the classic model (Figure 4) and the hybrid model (Figure 6).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparison between the hybrid CNN+SVM model and the classic CNN+Softmax model was based on descriptive statistics (precision, accuracy, recall, F1-score) and visual inspection of loss graphs.

HAM10000.notes: The hybrid CNN+SVM model achieved an improvement of approximately 1% over the classic CNN+Softmax model across all metrics. The hybrid model also demonstrated better convergence and less overfitting during training compared to the classic model.

architecture_used.main_model: Hybrid Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and Support Vector Machine (SVM)

architecture_details.classifier_head: L1-SVM (Support Vector Machine with L1 regularization loss function)

training_details.loss_function: L1-SVM (for the hybrid model); Cross-entropy (for the classic CNN+Softmax baseline)

preprocessing_techniques: Image loading and preparation for neural network input

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied 9 random rotations to each image in the BCC class to address class imbalance.
Applied 9 random zooms to each image in the BCC class to address class imbalance.
Applied vertical and horizontal turns (flips) to each image in the BCC class to address class imbalance.

conclusions: The proposed hybrid CNN+SVM model achieves better classification results for Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) compared to the classic CNN+Softmax model.
The hybrid model achieved average precision, accuracy, recall, and F1-score of approximately 96.20%, outperforming the classic model's average of approximately 95.66%.
The improvement of the hybrid model over the classic model is approximately 1% across all evaluated metrics.
The hybrid CNN+SVM model demonstrates better convergence and less overfitting during training compared to the classic model.
The hybrid model exhibits superior generalization capacity, as evidenced by more stable loss trends in the validation data compared to the classic model.
important_remarks: The study relies on a single dataset (HAM10000) with no external validation, limiting the assessment of generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.

The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for the performance difference between the hybrid CNN+SVM model and the classic CNN+Softmax model; the ~1% improvement is presented descriptively.

Data augmentation was applied exclusively to the BCC class to address class imbalance, which may introduce bias if the augmented samples do not adequately represent the true distribution of BCC lesions.
The input image resolution was reduced to 64x64 pixels, which may discard fine-grained dermatoscopic features critical for accurate diagnosis.
The model was evaluated only on a binary classification task (BCC vs. Non-BCC), ignoring the multi-class diagnostic utility of the HAM10000 dataset which contains seven distinct lesion types.

The authors are affiliated with universities in Peru, while the dataset originates from Austria and Australia, raising potential concerns regarding demographic and environmental bias in the training data relative to the authors' local context.
